ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'tea beverage' class (FOODON_03301103); no specific Indonesian red tea subclass exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers tea infusions broadly; no Indonesia-specific red tea code exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 16313001 refers to tea as a substance; no specific Indonesian red tea concept exists. Closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed Indonesian red tea, approximately 240 ml, served hot without milk or sugar unless specified.

nutrient_source.title: Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 14355)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Classic Indonesian Tea is a brewed black tea beverage served without milk or sugar. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for 'Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water' (NDB 14355 / FDC ID 171917) is used as the closest approved source match. Indonesian red tea (teh merah) is a black tea variety; nutrient profile is essentially identical to standard brewed black tea. Values represent plain brewed tea per 100 g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of beverages: plain brewed tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates; GI assigned by convention for zero-carbohydrate beverages
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed black tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates (carbohydrate_g = 0.0 per 100 g). The University of Sydney GI Database classifies zero-carbohydrate beverages such as plain tea and black coffee as having no measurable glycemic response. Per Project Veritas v2 zero-calorie beverage glycemic rule, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive sentinel for effectively zero-GI foods), gi_category is 'low', and glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g); no added salt
hypertension.guidance: Plain brewed Indonesian red tea contains negligible sodium (approximately 7 mg per 240 ml serving) and is safe for individuals managing hypertension; however, patients should avoid adding salt or high-sodium condiments and should monitor any added sugar or condensed milk if consumed in sweetened variants.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero glycemic carbohydrates; no measurable glycemic load when consumed plain
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain brewed Indonesian red tea has no glycemic carbohydrates and a glycemic load of zero per serving, making it a suitable beverage for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; patients should avoid sweetened or condensed-milk variants which significantly increase sugar and caloric load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero fat, zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol; contains tea polyphenols
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain brewed Indonesian red tea contains no fat, saturated fat, or cholesterol, and black tea polyphenols (theaflavins and thearubigins) have been associated with modest cardioprotective effects in observational studies; individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume plain tea freely but should avoid sweetened or full-fat milk variants.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Classic Indonesian Tea (Teh Merah Indonesia) is a traditional hot brewed beverage made by steeping Indonesian black tea leaves — commonly referred to as 'red tea' (teh merah) in Indonesian — in hot water. The drink is a staple across Indonesian households, warungs (small local eateries), and restaurants, and is closely associated with everyday Indonesian food culture. The tea is typically brewed strong and served hot, producing a deep amber-red liquor with a robust, slightly astringent flavour and a mild earthy aroma characteristic of Indonesian black tea varieties, which are primarily grown in Java and Sumatra. When served plain without sugar or milk, the beverage is essentially calorie-free, containing approximately 1 kcal per 100 g, with no meaningful protein, fat, or carbohydrate content. It provides trace amounts of potassium (37 mg per 100 g), magnesium (3 mg), and riboflavin (0.03 mg), along with naturally occurring tea polyphenols including theaflavins and thearubigins, which have been studied for antioxidant and cardioprotective properties. The glycemic index is effectively zero as the beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates, making it suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease when consumed without added sugar or condensed milk. In Singapore, Classic Indonesian Tea is served at Indonesian restaurants and food stalls such as Smile Martabak, often as an accompaniment to martabak (stuffed pancakes) or nasi padang. The drink may be served sweetened with sugar or with condensed milk in some variants, which substantially alters its nutritional profile.
common_user_queries: Is Indonesian red tea the same as black tea?
How many calories in Indonesian tea?
Is Indonesian tea good for diabetes?
Can I drink Indonesian tea if I have high blood pressure?
What does Indonesian red tea taste like?

confidence_reason: Classic Indonesian Tea is a plain brewed black tea beverage. No specific HPB Singapore Foodo or FAO/INFOODS SEA entry exists for this exact product. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 'Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water' (FDC ID 171917), which is the closest approved source match. The adaptation is highly defensible as Indonesian red tea (teh merah) is a black tea variety with an essentially identical nutrient profile to standard brewed black tea. Confidence is set to 'medium' rather than 'high' because no exact entry for Indonesian red tea was found in a primary regional source.
notes: 1. energy_kcal is 1.0 kcal per 100 g; this is the confirmed USDA value for plain brewed black tea and reflects the negligible caloric content of the beverage. 2. protein_g is naturally zero as plain brewed tea contains no protein by nature; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171917. 3. fat_g is naturally zero as plain brewed tea contains no fat by nature; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171917. 4. carbohydrate_g is naturally zero as plain brewed tea contains no glycemic carbohydrates by nature; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 171917. 5. sugar_g is naturally zero; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 6. dietary_fibre_g is naturally zero for a brewed liquid tea beverage; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 7. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as this beverage contains no glycemic carbohydrates; gi_category is 'low' per Project Veritas v2 zero-calorie beverage glycemic convention. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0. 8. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for plain brewed tea. 9. cholesterol_mg is 0.0; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 10. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg, and calcium_mg are 0.0; confirmed zero or below detection threshold per USDA FoodData Central for plain brewed black tea. 11. Nutrient values represent plain brewed tea without added sugar, milk, or condensed milk. Sweetened or milk-added variants will have substantially different nutritional profiles. 12. The food is served at Smile Martabak - Funan (field survey data); price SGD 3.50. 13. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301103 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are closest parent matches; no exact Indonesian red tea subclass exists in either ontology. 14. SNOMED CT 16313001 is a closest parent match for tea as a substance.
